title: Mascara on Polyester
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (wax + solvent + pigment). Mascara is formulated from: (1) WAX — beeswax, carnauba wax, or paraffin wax providing the coating structure, (2) SOLVENTS/OILS — mineral oil or volatile silicones as carriers, (3) PIGMENT — primarily carbon black (iron oxide for brown mascaras) plus film-forming polymers for waterproof formulas. Some mascaras contain biological proteins (keratin, collagen) as conditioning agents. The IRTA report specifically tested mascara and confirmed POG agents are effective. Carbon black is an inorganic pigment — it doesn't dissolve in solvents but becomes mechanically removable once the wax matrix is dissolved. On polyester, the oleophilic surface holds the waxy carrier tenaciously.
whyThisWorks: POG dissolves the wax matrix that encapsulates the carbon black pigment. Once the wax is gone, the carbon particles are freed and can be mechanically flushed out through the fabric and into the vacuum. This is primarily a mechanical extraction after chemical softening — the carbon itself doesn't dissolve, it's physically removed. Polyester handles this well because you can use strong solvents and vigorous mechanical action. The protein conditioning agents (if present) are addressed with NSD in the wetside step.

spottingProtocol.technique: Physical removal of bulk mascara before solvent
spottingProtocol.instruction: If mascara is dried, gently scrape off any flaking or crusted material with a spatula. Mascara typically dries to a brittle film — you can often remove 30-40% just by careful scraping. On polyester, moderate pressure is fine — the fiber is strong and resistant to abrasion. Don't scrape aggressively enough to pill the polyester surface.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull spatula, soft brush for dried particles

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or equivalent
spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve the wax matrix — primary chemical treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG agent generously to the mascara stain. The POG dissolves the wax beeswax/paraffin matrix and the oil carrier, releasing the carbon black pigment particles. Place garment face-down on spotting board over clean towel — you're pushing dissolved mascara through the fabric. Work with moderate to firm tamping — polyester handles mechanical action well. You should see dark color transferring to the towel. Flush with VDS. The IRTA report confirmed: POG agents (Soy Gold 2500, DPM) are effective on mascara. Repeat until dark transfer slows — typically 2-3 passes. Polyester is oleophilic, so expect the wax to cling harder than on cotton.
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board with vacuum, spotting brush, VDS, clean white towels

spottingProtocol.agent: VDS flush — clean flush for carbon particle extraction
spottingProtocol.technique: Mechanical flushing of freed carbon particles
spottingProtocol.instruction: After POG dissolves the wax, the freed carbon black particles need to be physically extracted. Flush generously with VDS while working the spotting board vacuum. The VDS carries carbon particles through the fabric. If using a steam gun for flushing, ensure all POG residue is removed first — don't mix dryside and wetside agents. Carbon black is pure carbon — it doesn't dissolve in anything. It can only be mechanically extracted once the wax prison is dissolved.

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD + water flush (for protein components and residual)
spottingProtocol.technique: Wetside cleanup — addresses biological protein components
spottingProtocol.instruction: Some mascaras contain keratin, collagen, or other protein-based conditioning agents. NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) emulsifies and removes these biological components plus any remaining oil residue. Apply NSD, work with brush, flush with warm water (polyester tolerates warm water well). If the mascara was 'fiber-lash' type containing nylon or silk fibers, these should have been mechanically removed in step 1 — if any remain, pick out with tweezers.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 3% (for brown/colored mascara residue only)
spottingProtocol.technique: Mild bleaching for iron oxide pigment — if applicable
spottingProtocol.instruction: Skip this step for black mascara (carbon black doesn't respond to bleach — it's elemental carbon). For brown mascara, the pigment is iron oxide — apply 3% hydrogen peroxide with a drop of ammonia. On polyester, you can safely use 6% peroxide if 3% isn't sufficient. Polyester has excellent bleach tolerance. Flush thoroughly after treatment.

Apply dilute acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize any residual alkalinity. Flush thoroughly with clean water. Air dry and inspect. Polyester dries quickly. Once dry, check under good light — carbon black stains can be deceptively faint when wet and darken when dry. If shadow remains, repeat from step 2.

homeSolutions: Let mascara dry completely if still wet — dried mascara is easier to remove than wet mascara. Once dry, gently brush off the flakes over a trash can.
Apply a few drops of Dawn dish soap directly to the remaining stain. The surfactant breaks down the waxy binder. Work with an old toothbrush in gentle circles. Rinse with warm water.

For waterproof mascara: apply a small amount of coconut oil or olive oil to the stain first — oil dissolves oil. Let sit 5 minutes, then apply dish soap over the oil to emulsify both. Rinse with warm water.
Launder in warm water with regular detergent. Inspect before drying — heat can set any remaining carbon into the polyester. If stain persists, repeat the dish soap treatment before re-washing.
materialWarnings: Polyester is oleophilic — it attracts and holds waxy/oily stains. Expect 2-3 treatment passes minimum.
Carbon black does NOT dissolve in any solvent — it must be mechanically flushed out after the wax matrix is dissolved.
Waterproof mascaras contain additional polymer sealants — they require more aggressive POG treatment and possibly amyl acetate to dissolve the waterproofing polymer.
Heat permanently sets carbon particles into polyester — no dryer until confirmed clean.
Some mascaras contain iron oxide (brown shades) — this is a mineral pigment that may require different chemistry than carbon black.
escalation: If mascara persists after standard POG treatment: the formula may be waterproof with a silicone or dimethicone polymer sealant. Escalate to amyl acetate to dissolve the waterproofing polymer, then re-treat with POG for the wax. For extremely set mascara on polyester where carbon is embedded in the fiber matrix, ultrasonic cleaning or extended POG soaking may be required. On polyester, you can safely try acetone as a final solvent escalation — polyester rates Grade B with acetone.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: Primary agent — dissolves the wax matrix to free carbon pigment. IRTA-tested on mascara.

professional.name: Amyl Acetate (Banana Oil)
professional.amazonSearchQuery: amyl acetate banana oil spotting solvent
professional.note: Escalation for waterproof mascara polymer sealants.

professional.name: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent)
professional.note: Addresses protein conditioning agents and residual oil in mascara.

household.name: Dawn Dish Soap (Original Blue)
household.note: Effective on mascara's oil and wax components. Apply directly to stain.

household.name: Coconut Oil
household.note: Oil dissolves oil — effective pre-treatment for waterproof mascara. Follow with dish soap.

customerExplanation: Mascara is primarily wax with carbon black pigment suspended inside it — think of carbon particles trapped in a waxy shell. Our treatment dissolves the waxy shell with professional solvents, then flushes the freed carbon particles out through the fabric using vacuum extraction. The carbon itself doesn't dissolve — it has to be physically removed. On polyester, the waxy carrier sticks more than on other fabrics, so it takes a few treatment passes.
commonMistakes: Rubbing wet mascara — this smears the wax-carbon mixture across a larger area. Let it dry first, then treat. | Expecting carbon to dissolve — carbon black is elemental carbon, the same stuff as charcoal. No solvent dissolves it. You dissolve the WAX, then flush the carbon out mechanically. | Skipping the flush step — dissolving the wax without thorough flushing just re-deposits carbon elsewhere in the fabric. | Expecting bleach to work on black mascara — bleach breaks chromophores (dye molecules). Carbon black has no chromophore to break.
scienceNote: Carbon black (CAS 1333-86-4) is the most chemically inert common pigment — it's essentially pure amorphous carbon with no functional groups to attack chemically. This is why mascara stain removal is fundamentally a mechanical problem after the wax dissolution: you're extracting solid particles, not dissolving a dye. The spotting board vacuum is critical for this reason — it provides the extraction force that home methods lack. This also explains why mascara stains respond well to repeated treatment: each pass dissolves more wax, freeing more carbon for extraction.
callPro: Fresh mascara on polyester is treatable at home with dish soap. For waterproof formulas or set mascara, professional treatment with the spotting board provides the vacuum extraction needed to physically remove carbon particles that home methods can't reach.
difficulty_factors: Standard (non-waterproof) mascara responds well to POG treatment
Polyester is durable — tolerates aggressive chemistry and mechanical action
Waterproof mascara adds difficulty due to additional polymer sealant
solventNote: For PERC shops: PERC has excellent compatibility with polyester and good solvency for mascara wax. Pre-spot on board for heavy mascara, or machine process for light smears. For HC shops: hydrocarbon solvent effective but less aggressive — extend board time for waterproof formulas.
